(20) Learning Hexo from Scratch - NexT Theme: Plugins You Can Install
A few NexT plugins I use myself, plus some that are worth knowing about.
Hexo doesn't have local search built in, so you'll need a plugin. Most themes recommend
to generate a search index. But if you're on NexT, I'd recommend
instead — more stable, and you'll avoid a few landmines.
Install is straightforward — run this from your Hexo project:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
is optimised for NexT, you'll find the config in
language-yml shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

# Local Search
# Dependencies: https://github.com/theme-next/hexo-generator-searchdb
local_search:
  enable: false
  # If auto, trigger search by changing input.
  # If manual, trigger search by pressing enter key or search button.
  trigger: auto
  # Show top n results per article, show all results by setting to -1
  top_n_per_article: 1
  # Unescape html strings to the readable one.
  unescape: false
  # Preload the search data when the page loads.
  preload: false
# If auto, trigger search by changing input.
# If manual, trigger search by pressing enter key or search button.
# Show top n results per article, show all results by setting to -1
# Unescape html strings to the readable one.
# Preload the search data when the page loads.
and your site has local search.
Older versions used to ask whether you wanted to generate xml or json. Newer NexT just uses json since the file is smaller.

Bookmark is a small plugin that remembers where you left off on a page — one click and it jumps you back to where you were last reading. It's not bundled with NexT and it's not on npm, so you'll have to clone it from GitHub. From your Hexo project,
first and then run the clone:
// Make sure you're inside the next theme folder before running the next command
Once installed, clicking the bookmark icon on the page will jump you back to your last scroll position:
(If I remember right, testing on localhost didn't seem to work — try it on the deployed site.)
A reading progress bar plugin — basically lets readers see how much of the article is left. Not much else to say, so here's the install:
Now when you read an article, you'll see a progress bar at the top:
That's a wrap for today's Ironman post. I personally don't install many NexT plugins — too many slows down compile times, so be picky about what you actually need.
See you in the next one.
